query:
INSERT INTO safetyTopics (title, content, category, isActive, createdBy) VALUES
('Fall Protection Basics', 'Key Points:
• Always use fall protection when working at heights of 6 feet or more
• Inspect harnesses, lanyards, and anchor points before each use
• Never alter or remove guardrails without authorization
• Know the difference between fall arrest, fall restraint, and positioning systems
• Report damaged equipment immediately

Discussion: What fall hazards exist on our current job site? How can we eliminate or reduce them?', 'fall_protection', true, 1),

('Ladder Safety', 'Key Points:
• Inspect ladders before each use — check rungs, rails, and feet
• Maintain 3 points of contact at all times
• Set up on stable, level ground with proper angle (4:1 ratio)
• Never stand on the top two rungs of a stepladder
• Face the ladder when climbing up or down
• Do not carry heavy or bulky items while climbing

Discussion: Are all ladders on site in good condition? Who needs a refresher on proper setup?', 'fall_protection', true, 1),

('Scaffold Safety', 'Key Points:
• Only trained workers may erect, dismantle, or alter scaffolds
• Inspect scaffolds daily before use
• Guardrails required on all open sides above 10 feet
• Never exceed the scaffold load capacity
• Lock all casters/wheels before climbing
• Use proper access — never climb cross braces

Discussion: Review current scaffold setups on site. Are all guardrails and toe boards in place?', 'scaffolding', true, 1),

('Electrical Safety Awareness', 'Key Points:
• Assume all wires are live until verified otherwise
• Use GFCI protection for all temporary power
• Keep electrical panels accessible — 36 inch clearance
• Never use damaged cords or plug adapters
• Lock Out / Tag Out before working on electrical systems
• Water and electricity do not mix — keep cords dry

Discussion: Where are the electrical hazards on our current project? Is all temporary power GFCI protected?', 'electrical', true, 1),

('Lockout/Tagout Procedures', 'Key Points:
• LOTO protects workers from unexpected energization
• Only authorized employees may perform LOTO
• Follow the 6 steps: Prepare, Shut Down, Isolate, Apply LOTO, Release Stored Energy, Verify
• Each worker applies their own lock — never remove someone else''s lock
• Communicate with all affected employees before and after

Discussion: Who on our crew is LOTO authorized? What equipment on site requires LOTO?', 'electrical', true, 1),

('Excavation & Trench Safety', 'Key Points:
• Trenches 5 feet or deeper require protective systems (sloping, shoring, or shielding)
• A competent person must inspect trenches daily and after rain
• Keep spoil piles at least 2 feet from trench edges
• Never enter an unprotected trench
• Have a means of egress (ladder/ramp) within 25 feet of workers
• Watch for underground utilities — call 811 before digging

Discussion: Review our current excavation plan. Is the competent person identified?', 'excavation', true, 1),

('PPE — Hard Hats & Head Protection', 'Key Points:
• Hard hats required in all active work areas
• Inspect for cracks, dents, and UV damage before each use
• Replace after any impact, even if no visible damage
• Do not modify, drill, or paint hard hats
• Wear the brim forward unless designed for reverse wear
• Replace suspension systems annually

Discussion: Is everyone wearing their hard hat correctly? Any damaged hard hats that need replacement?', 'ppe', true, 1),

('PPE — Eye & Face Protection', 'Key Points:
• Safety glasses required at all times on active job sites
• Use face shields for grinding, cutting, and chipping
• Tinted lenses for outdoor work, clear for indoor
• Keep lenses clean — scratched lenses reduce visibility
• Prescription safety glasses must meet ANSI Z87.1 standards

Discussion: Does everyone have proper eye protection for their current task?', 'ppe', true, 1),

('PPE — Hand Protection & Gloves', 'Key Points:
• Select gloves appropriate for the hazard (cut, chemical, heat, impact)
• Inspect gloves before use — check for tears, holes, or wear
• Never wear gloves near rotating machinery
• Cut-resistant gloves for handling sheet metal, glass, or sharp materials
• Chemical-resistant gloves when handling solvents, adhesives, or concrete

Discussion: Are crew members using the right glove type for their current tasks?', 'ppe', true, 1),

('PPE — Hearing Protection', 'Key Points:
• Hearing protection required when noise exceeds 85 dB (8-hour average)
• Common loud tools: concrete saws, jackhammers, powder-actuated tools, grinders
• Earplugs must be properly inserted — roll, pull, hold
• Earmuffs must seal completely around the ear
• Noise-induced hearing loss is permanent and irreversible

Discussion: Which tasks on our current job require hearing protection?', 'ppe', true, 1),

('Heat Illness Prevention', 'Key Points:
• Drink water frequently — at least 1 cup every 15-20 minutes
• Take breaks in shaded or cool areas
• Know the signs: heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, dizziness, confusion
• Acclimatize new workers — start with lighter workloads
• Use buddy system — watch each other for symptoms
• Call 911 immediately for heat stroke (confusion, loss of consciousness, hot dry skin)

Discussion: Where are our water and shade stations? Does everyone know the emergency plan?', 'heat_stress', true, 1),

('Cold Weather Safety', 'Key Points:
• Dress in layers — moisture-wicking base, insulating middle, wind-resistant outer
• Protect extremities: insulated gloves, warm socks, face covering
• Watch for frostbite signs: numbness, white/grayish skin, hard/waxy feeling
• Watch for hypothermia signs: shivering, confusion, slurred speech, drowsiness
• Take warm-up breaks regularly
• Keep extra dry clothing available

Discussion: Is everyone dressed appropriately for today''s conditions?', 'general', true, 1),

('Fire Prevention on Construction Sites', 'Key Points:
• Keep fire extinguishers within 100 feet of hot work areas
• Store flammable materials away from ignition sources
• No smoking near flammable materials or fuels
• Hot work permits required for welding, cutting, and brazing
• Maintain clear access to fire extinguishers and exits
• Know the location of the nearest fire extinguisher and how to use it (PASS: Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep)

Discussion: Where are our fire extinguishers? When was the last inspection?', 'fire', true, 1),

('Hot Work Safety', 'Key Points:
• Hot work permit required before welding, cutting, or brazing
• Fire watch required during and 30 minutes after hot work
• Remove or protect combustibles within 35 feet
• Inspect equipment before use — check hoses, regulators, and connections
• Never weld on containers that held flammable materials
• Ensure adequate ventilation

Discussion: Who is designated as fire watch today? Is the hot work permit posted?', 'fire', true, 1),

('Silica Dust Exposure', 'Key Points:
• Silica dust is created when cutting, grinding, or drilling concrete, brick, or stone
• Exposure can cause silicosis — a serious, irreversible lung disease
• Use wet cutting methods whenever possible
• Use vacuum dust collection systems on tools
• Wear N95 or P100 respirator when engineering controls are insufficient
• Never dry sweep silica dust — use wet methods or HEPA vacuum

Discussion: What tasks today will generate silica dust? Are controls in place?', 'chemical', true, 1),

('Hazard Communication (HazCom)', 'Key Points:
• All chemicals on site must have Safety Data Sheets (SDS) available
• Read labels before using any chemical product
• Know the GHS pictograms and what they mean
• Store chemicals properly — separate incompatible materials
• Use required PPE listed on the SDS
• Report any unlabeled containers immediately

Discussion: Where is our SDS binder? Does everyone know how to read an SDS?', 'chemical', true, 1),

('Crane & Rigging Safety', 'Key Points:
• Only qualified operators may operate cranes
• Never stand under a suspended load
• Use a qualified signal person for all lifts
• Inspect all rigging equipment before each use
• Know the load capacity — never exceed it
• Maintain safe distance from power lines (minimum 10 feet for lines under 50kV)

Discussion: Review today''s lift plan. Is the rigging inspected and rated for the load?', 'equipment', true, 1),

('Forklift & Equipment Safety', 'Key Points:
• Only trained and authorized operators may operate forklifts
• Perform daily pre-operation inspection
• Wear seatbelt at all times
• Travel with forks lowered (4-6 inches above ground)
• Sound horn at intersections and blind spots
• Never allow riders on forks or attachments
• Maintain 3 feet clearance from edges and drop-offs

Discussion: Are all operators on site properly certified?', 'equipment', true, 1),

('Power Tool Safety', 'Key Points:
• Inspect all power tools before use
• Use the right tool for the job
• Never remove or bypass safety guards
• Disconnect power before changing blades or bits
• Secure workpieces with clamps — never hold by hand
• Keep cords away from cutting path and heat sources
• Wear appropriate PPE: eye, ear, and hand protection

Discussion: Are all guards in place on our power tools? Any tools that need repair?', 'equipment', true, 1),

('Housekeeping & Slip/Trip/Fall Prevention', 'Key Points:
• Keep walkways and work areas clear of debris
• Clean up spills immediately
• Store materials neatly — don''t block exits or walkways
• Use proper lighting in all work areas
• Cover or barricade floor openings
• Secure loose cords and hoses
• Report hazards immediately

Discussion: Walk the site — what housekeeping issues need attention right now?', 'general', true, 1),

('Struck-By Hazards', 'Key Points:
• Struck-by incidents are one of OSHA''s Fatal Four
• Wear high-visibility vests near equipment and traffic
• Never position yourself between moving equipment and fixed objects
• Secure tools and materials at height — use tool lanyards
• Barricade overhead work areas
• Be aware of swinging loads during crane operations

Discussion: Where are the struck-by hazards on our current site?', 'general', true, 1),

('Caught-In/Between Hazards', 'Key Points:
• Never reach into unguarded moving machinery
• Avoid loose clothing, jewelry, and long hair near rotating equipment
• Use LOTO procedures before maintenance
• Stay clear of trenching and excavation collapse zones
• Never enter between a vehicle and a loading dock without wheel chocks
• Be aware of pinch points when assembling or disassembling forms

Discussion: What caught-in/between hazards exist on our current project?', 'general', true, 1),

('Concrete & Masonry Safety', 'Key Points:
• Wet concrete is caustic — causes chemical burns
• Wear waterproof boots, gloves, and long sleeves when working with concrete
• Wash skin immediately if contact occurs
• Use knee boards when finishing — avoid kneeling in wet concrete
• Ensure formwork is properly braced before pouring
• Never ride concrete buckets

Discussion: Is everyone wearing proper PPE for today''s concrete work?', 'chemical', true, 1),

('Confined Space Entry', 'Key Points:
• A confined space has limited entry/exit, is not designed for continuous occupancy, and may have hazardous atmosphere
• Permit required before entry into permit-required confined spaces
• Test atmosphere before entry: oxygen, flammables, toxics
• Have a trained attendant stationed outside at all times
• Rescue plan must be in place before entry begins
• Never enter to rescue — call trained rescue team

Discussion: Are there any confined spaces on our current project?', 'general', true, 1),

('Back Injury Prevention & Proper Lifting', 'Key Points:
• Plan the lift — know the weight and path
• Lift with your legs, not your back
• Keep the load close to your body
• Avoid twisting while carrying
• Get help for loads over 50 lbs
• Use mechanical aids when available (dollies, carts, hoists)
• Stretch before heavy lifting tasks

Discussion: What heavy lifting tasks are planned today? Can we use mechanical aids?', 'general', true, 1),

('Emergency Action Plan', 'Key Points:
• Know the emergency assembly point for this job site
• Know the location of first aid kits and AED
• Know who the designated first aid responders are
• In case of fire: evacuate, call 911, do not fight large fires
• In case of injury: secure the scene, call for help, provide first aid
• Keep emergency numbers posted: 911, project manager, safety officer

Discussion: Where is our assembly point? Does everyone know the emergency contacts?', 'general', true, 1),

('Respiratory Protection', 'Key Points:
• Respirators required when engineering controls cannot reduce exposure
• Must be medically cleared and fit-tested before use
• Select the right respirator for the hazard (dust, fumes, chemicals)
• Inspect before each use — check straps, valves, and filters
• Clean and store properly after use
• Facial hair prevents proper seal — must be clean-shaven in seal area

Discussion: Who on our crew needs respiratory protection today? Is everyone fit-tested?', 'ppe', true, 1),

('Nail Gun Safety', 'Key Points:
• Treat nail guns like firearms — never point at anyone
• Use sequential trigger mode (not bump/contact trigger) for precision
• Keep finger off trigger when not actively nailing
• Wear safety glasses at all times
• Be aware of what is behind the work surface
• Never disable safety mechanisms
• Disconnect when not in use, clearing jams, or climbing

Discussion: Are all nail guns on site in sequential trigger mode?', 'equipment', true, 1),

('Working Near Utilities', 'Key Points:
• Call 811 at least 48 hours before any digging
• Maintain safe distances from overhead power lines
• Use non-conductive tools near electrical utilities
• Know the location of underground gas, water, and sewer lines
• Report any utility strikes immediately — evacuate if gas
• Use hand digging within 24 inches of marked utilities

Discussion: Have we called 811 for this project? Are all utilities marked?', 'electrical', true, 1),

('Stairway Safety During Construction', 'Key Points:
• Stairways with 4 or more risers must have handrails
• Keep stairways clear of tools, materials, and debris
• Temporary stairs must support 5x the anticipated load
• Adequate lighting required on all stairways
• Metal pan stairs must be filled or have temporary treads
• Use stairways — do not climb on structural members

Discussion: Are all temporary stairways on site properly railed and clear?', 'fall_protection', true, 1),

('Daily Stretch & Flex', 'Key Points:
• Stretching before work reduces muscle strains and sprains
• Focus on: neck, shoulders, back, hamstrings, wrists, and ankles
• Hold each stretch for 15-30 seconds — no bouncing
• Breathe normally during stretches
• Stretch again after breaks and before heavy tasks
• Report any pain or discomfort — early intervention prevents serious injury

Let''s stretch together as a crew before starting work today.', 'general', true, 1);

mysql --batch --raw --column-names --default-character-set=utf8mb4 --host gateway04.us-east-1.prod.aws.tidbcloud.com --port 4000 --user 378BqZUfvZWHcSK.root --database dNJxctHZxj6wCg3jq4j4kh --execute
